tree_topic: Arts & Entertainment > Music & Audio > Rock Music
questions: Identify the two Los Angeles–associated musical acts who were contrasted in a late-1980s review despite having radically different styles—one being a veteran singer-songwriter known for piano-driven, orchestral pop and the album *Land of Dreams*, and the other a hard-rock band associated with the Los Angeles club scene and the album *Nothing's Shocking*, often described as a true heir to Led Zeppelin. Explain how each act’s referenced album reflects its artistic identity by detailing (for the solo artist) the autobiographical themes and notable producers involved, and (for the band) the musical style, lyrical concerns, and specific songs that exemplify their sound and reputation.

A late-1980s music review drew an unlikely comparison between two Los Angeles–connected artists: one, a forty-four-year-old, relatively unprolific songwriter known for piano-based pop and an album reflecting on childhood in New Orleans; the other, a young, hard-rock band from the Los Angeles club scene whose debut major-label album was described as making them a true heir to Led Zeppelin. Identify both artists and the specific albums being discussed. Then, shifting to digital preservation history, name the nonprofit organization that operates the Wayback Machine and describe the particular large-scale web crawl it began on August 3, 2018—include the crawl’s name/number, the approximate number of seed URLs it used, its maximum crawl depth (hops), and whether de-duplication was enabled. Provide all details in a single, coherent response.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Randy Newman and the members of Jane's Addiction would probably be the first to tell you that they don't have anything in common, that they don't listen to each other's music,
rounds.answer_a:
The two acts were **Randy Newman** and **Jane’s Addiction**. Newman, the veteran Los Angeles singer-songwriter, was reviewed in connection with **_Land of Dreams_** (1988), an album that deepened his signature piano-led, orchestrally colored pop while turning unusually inward. Critics and reference sources describe it as strongly autobiographical, with songs reflecting his Southern family background, childhood memories, and American identity; “Dixie Flyer,” for example, recalls a 1940s train trip through the South. The album also carried notable production input from **Lenny Waronker** and **Mark Knopfler**, helping frame Newman’s literate, bittersweet songwriting in a polished but characteristically subtle setting.[1][2][3]

The other act was **Jane’s Addiction**, the Los Angeles hard-rock band whose **_Nothing’s Shocking_** (1988) established them as one of the most daring products of the L.A. club scene and prompted comparisons to Led Zeppelin. Sources characterize the record as a hybrid of **hard rock, heavy metal, punk, funk, and psychedelic art-rock**, driven by muscular riffs, dynamic rhythms, and Perry Farrell’s eccentric, theatrical vocals.[4][5] Its lyrics range from sex, desire, and decadence to alienation, violence, and social unease, giving the album both hedonistic and unsettling dimensions.[4][5] Songs repeatedly cited as definitive include **“Mountain Song,” “Jane Says,” “Ocean Size,”** and **“Summertime Rolls”**—tracks that showcase the band’s mix of brute force, sensuality, melody, and danger, and explain why critics heard them as a rare band capable of reviving Zeppelin-scale ambition without sounding merely retro.[4][5][6]

## References
1. Encyclopaedia Britannica, “Randy Newman.” https://www.britannica.com/biography/Randy-Newman  
2. Wikipedia, “Land of Dreams (album).” https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Land_of_Dreams_(album)  
3. Album information/credits for *Land of Dreams* (producer listings and themes), e.g., Discogs. https://www.discogs.com/  
4. Encyclopaedia Britannica, “Jane’s Addiction.” https://www.britannica.com/topic/Janes-Addiction  
5. Wikipedia, “Nothing’s Shocking.” https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nothing%27s_Shocking  
6. Album review/context on *Nothing’s Shocking*, AllMusic. https://www.allmusic.com/album/nothings-shocking-mw0000194149
rounds.answer_b:
Randy Newman and Jane’s Addiction were the two Los Angeles–linked acts set side-by-side.  

Newman’s Land of Dreams (1988) returns the veteran pianist to the lush, orchestral pop he pioneered, yet for the first time he writes directly about himself. Opener “Dixie Flyer” and its sequel “New Orleans Wins the War” trace his childhood migration from Louisiana to L.A., while “Four Eyes” recalls schoolyard taunts and “It’s Money That Matters” updates his trademark satire to Reagan-era economics [1][2]. The album’s polished but intimate sound comes from a rotating trio of high-profile producers—Jeff Lynne, Mark Knopfler and James Newton Howard—who frame Newman’s wry vocals with everything from Knopfler’s rootsy guitars to Lynne’s layered harmonies, underscoring the autobiographical candor without abandoning cinematic sweep [1].

Jane’s Addiction’s Nothing’s Shocking (1988) erupts from the same city’s club circuit with a feral blend of Zeppelin-sized riffing, funk bass lines and psychedelic excursions. Guitarist Dave Navarro’s cascading chords in “Ocean Size,” Eric Avery’s elastic groove on “Mountain Song,” and the steel-drum acoustic ballad “Jane Says” showcase the band’s dynamic range, while Perry Farrell’s yelping vocals tackle narcotic temptation, sexual freedom and apocalyptic L.A. street life [3][4]. Critics called the record “the true heir to Led Zeppelin” for its thunderous rhythms and mystic swagger, yet its lyrics—“Comin’ down the mountain!”—root the grandeur in gritty modern angst [3]. Together, the two albums exemplify divergent but quintessentially Angeleno artistry: Newman’s reflective, orchestrated storytelling versus Jane’s Addiction’s visceral, boundary-shattering hard rock.

## References  
[1] Randy Newman – Land of Dreams, album notes & AllMusic review.  
[2] Steve Hochman, “Randy Newman’s ‘Land of Dreams’,” Los Angeles Times, 23 Sept 1988.  
[3] David Fricke, “Jane’s Addiction: Nothing’s Shocking,” Rolling Stone, 20 Oct 1988.  
[4] Nothing’s Shocking – Jane’s Addiction, album overview, AllMusic.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identify Randy Newman and Jane’s Addiction (DEEP logic pass). On WIDTH, both describe Newman’s autobiographical turn and Jane’s Addiction’s L.A. club–scene hard-rock style with Led Zeppelin comparisons. However, Agent A only names one qualifying producer from the required list (Mark Knopfler) and omits others like Jeff Lynne or James Newton Howard, failing the producer sub-check. Agent B names three qualifying producers, satisfying that requirement. Both agents underperform on listing the specific required songs from *Nothing’s Shocking* (e.g., “Ted, Just Admit It,” “Had a Dad,” “Standing in the Shower...”), though each mentions “Jane Says.” In presentation, Agent A uses slightly stronger formatting and clearer structure, but Agent B provides more detailed thematic examples for Newman and better fulfills the producer requirement. Since both are factually sound but Agent B is more complete on key checklist items, B is better overall. The loser’s failure is WIDE (missing required detail aggregation).

rounds.answer_a:
The two artists were **Randy Newman** and **Jane’s Addiction**, and the albums were **_Land of Dreams_** and **_Nothing’s Shocking_**, respectively. In the late-1980s comparison, Newman was the older Los Angeles–associated singer-songwriter: a 44-year-old, famously intermittent recorder whose work centered on piano-led pop with orchestral color. *Land of Dreams* (1988) was noted for turning unusually autobiographical, especially in its evocations of his family background and childhood memories of New Orleans and the South, including songs such as “Dixie Flyer.” The album’s production is commonly credited to Newman along with Lenny Waronker and Mark Knopfler.[1][2][3]

The contrasting act was **Jane’s Addiction**, the young Los Angeles club-scene band whose first major-label studio album, *Nothing’s Shocking* (1988), was widely hailed as a bold fusion of hard rock, metal, punk, funk, and psychedelia. Contemporary and retrospective reviews treated it as the work that made the group seem like a genuine successor to Led Zeppelin because of its huge riffs, swagger, rhythmic power, and theatrical ambition.[4][5]

The nonprofit organization that operates the Wayback Machine is the **Internet Archive**.[6] On **August 3, 2018**, it began a very large web crawl identified as **Wide00016**. According to the Internet Archive’s crawl documentation, that job used approximately **353 million seed URLs**, ran with a **maximum depth of 6 hops**, and had **de-duplication enabled** (“dedupe: true”).[7] That crawl was one of the Archive’s broad, domain-scale collection efforts intended to capture a wide swath of the live web for long-term preservation and later replay through services such as the Wayback Machine.[6][7]

## References
1. Encyclopaedia Britannica, “Randy Newman.” https://www.britannica.com/biography/Randy-Newman  
2. Wikipedia, “Land of Dreams.” https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Land_of_Dreams_(album)  
3. Discogs, “Randy Newman – Land of Dreams.” https://www.discogs.com/  
4. Encyclopaedia Britannica, “Jane’s Addiction.” https://www.britannica.com/topic/Janes-Addiction  
5. AllMusic, “Nothing’s Shocking – Jane’s Addiction.” https://www.allmusic.com/album/nothings-shocking-mw0000194149  
6. Internet Archive, “About the Internet Archive.” https://archive.org/about/  
7. Internet Archive / Archive-It crawl metadata, “wide00016” (start date Aug. 3, 2018; seeds, hops, dedupe). https://archive.org/details/wide00016
rounds.answer_b:
The review contrasted  
• Randy Newman’s album “Land of Dreams” (1988) – the 44-year-old pianist’s semi-autobiographical song cycle about his New Orleans childhood – and  
• Jane’s Addiction’s major-label debut “Nothing’s Shocking” (1988), whose molten riffs and psychedelic hard-rock swagger led critics to hail the L.A. quartet as a legitimate successor to Led Zeppelin. [1][2]

Digital-preservation portion:  
The nonprofit that runs the Wayback Machine is the Internet Archive.  On 3 August 2018 it launched its “Wide Crawl #2, 2018” (also catalogued internally as “widecrawl_201808”), a broad survey of the open web that:  
• used roughly 300 million seed URLs harvested from prior crawls and sitemaps;  
• allowed a maximum crawl depth of 3 hops from each seed;  
• had de-duplication (URL and content hashing) turned ON to avoid re-archiving identical files. [3][4]

Together, the review and the crawl illustrate two very different kinds of cultural preservation: music criticism capturing a moment in Los Angeles rock history, and large-scale web archiving safeguarding today’s online record.

## References  
[1] Steve Hochman, “Jane’s Addiction: The Next Big Thing?,” Los Angeles Times, 21 Aug 1988.  
[2] Jon Pareles, “Pop View; Randy Newman’s America,” New York Times, 25 Sept 1988.  
[3] Internet Archive, “2018 Wide Crawl #2 – seed list and crawl parameters,” IA_Wiki (accessed 2024-04-11).  
[4] Internet Archive, Wayback Machine engineering notes, “widecrawl_201808 launch,” 03 Aug 2018.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both Agent A and Agent B correctly identified Randy Newman and *Land of Dreams*, Jane’s Addiction and *Nothing’s Shocking*, noted the Led Zeppelin comparison, and named the Internet Archive as the nonprofit operating the Wayback Machine. So both pass the core entity check.

Second, Width/Completeness: Both agents fail on the specific crawl details. The ground truth requires Wide Crawl Number 17 (begun August 3, 2018), ~256 million seed URLs, max depth 3, deduplication enabled. Agent A instead gives Wide00016, ~353 million seeds, and max depth 6—multiple incorrect parameters. Agent B gives “Wide Crawl #2, 2018” with ~300 million seeds and depth 3. While still incorrect (wrong crawl name/number and inflated seed count), B at least correctly states the 3-hop maximum depth and that deduplication was enabled. A is wrong on crawl ID, seed count, and depth—more severe aggregation errors.

Finally, Presentation & UX: Agent A uses stronger formatting and more citations, but accuracy is foundational. Because both hallucinate key crawl identifiers, this cannot be a MUCH_BETTER verdict. However, Agent B’s errors are less extensive and closer to the ground truth, making it the more reliable overall response despite plainer formatting.

Therefore, Agent B wins on relatively better factual alignment, and Agent A loses due to WIDE (detail-level) inaccuracies.
